food_subgroup: Poultry by-products — cartilage, deep-fried or stir-fried

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried or stir-fried chicken cartilage. FOODON_03302051 (chicken food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers chicken meat and offal broadly; no specific code exists for chicken cartilage preparations. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried chicken cartilage as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of salt and pepper chicken cartilage is approximately 150 g, representing a shared appetiser or side dish portion as commonly served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, separable fat, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with cartilage-specific collagen protein profile from USDA Foundation Foods chicken entries and peer-reviewed cartilage composition literature
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for fried chicken cartilage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken skin/connective tissue (FDC ID 171477 and related entries) as a fat and energy anchor; (2) published cartilage composition data indicating high collagen protein (~15–20 g/100g raw), low fat (~3–5 g/100g), and negligible carbohydrate; (3) adjustment for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–12 g fat added per 100g) and salt seasoning (~400–600 mg sodium per 100g finished product). The resulting profile is a conservative mid-range estimate. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/coated fried meat products (GI ~30–40)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried chicken cartilage. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous fried coated meat/poultry products (GI range 30–40) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The low carbohydrate content (5.5 g/100g, primarily from flour coating) and high protein/fat ratio suppress glycemic response. GL per 150g serving = (35 × 5.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.9 g, classified low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100g; ~780 mg per 150g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as a single 150g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and cumulative intake from other meal components may easily exceed safe thresholds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and low carbohydrate load of this dish make it relatively suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high fat content from deep-frying warrants attention to overall caloric intake and frequency of consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.2 g/100g) and elevated sodium (~520 mg/100g) from deep-frying and salt seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its combined saturated fat and sodium load; deep-frying increases total fat significantly, and regular consumption may contribute to adverse lipid profiles and blood pressure elevation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salt and Pepper Chicken Cartilage (Vietnamese: Sụn gà rang muối; Chinese: 盐酥鸡软骨) is a popular fried poultry dish in which chicken cartilage — typically sourced from the sternum or keel bone — is marinated, coated in a light seasoned batter of cornstarch or flour, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is finished with a dry stir-fry in a wok with garlic, salt, white pepper, and black pepper, producing a highly aromatic, crunchy exterior with a chewy, collagen-rich interior. The texture is distinctive: the cartilage provides a satisfying crunch and chew that differs markedly from standard chicken meat dishes. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 17.5 g/100g), moderate-to-high in fat (approximately 17 g/100g) due to deep-frying, and low in carbohydrates (~5.5 g/100g). The collagen content of cartilage is notable, providing glycine and proline-rich protein. Sodium content is elevated (~520 mg/100g) due to salt seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is commonly served as an appetiser or side dish in Vietnamese and pan-Asian restaurants across Singapore, and is culturally associated with Vietnamese street food traditions adapted for the Singaporean dining context.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried chicken cartilage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken connective tissue entries combined with published cartilage composition literature. GI is an analogous estimate from comparable fried coated meat products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and multi-source, but direct measurement data are absent.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the reference sources for this food type and cartilage-specific data are absent. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for fried chicken cartilage or analogous entries. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for fried chicken cartilage. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. The dish is listed under Singapore cuisine because it is served in Singapore by a Vietnamese restaurant (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road), representing a Vietnamese dish adapted for the Singapore market. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the restaurant directly. The price of SGD 20.00 per serving is preserved from the GrabFood source record for reference only and is not used in nutritional calculations.
